summary: Twins are classified as high-risk infants because they face increased complexity during delivery with heightened potential for multiple health issues such as twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome; these conditions make them more vulnerable than singletons without additional compounding maternal or pregnancy-related problems like those mentioned but unassociated directly with being at elevated immediate neonatal/infant mortality/survival concerns alone among otherwise healthy pregnancies. Agent2 concludes based largely upon documented prevalence data indicating greater incidence of adverse outcomes specifically linked to multiplicity rather than isolated instances from non-multiples scenarios presented here which lack direct correlation proving independent 'high-risks'. To reiterate succinctly while focusing strictly clinically pertinent details per request: Twin births inherently pose substantial physiological stressors leading towards categorizing all twins together under "high-risk" status irrespective if individual components within pair exhibit no apparent abnormalities beyond mere fact their gestation involves dual entities necessitating closer monitoring throughout prenatal period into postnatal care phases where interventions may be required preemptively addressing anticipated vulnerabilities characteristic across both members comprising each set regardless whether one exhibits signs symptomatic deviations requiring separate attention apart from general category designation itself applicable broadly speaking universally amongst solitary fetus scenario typically encountered instead when considering typical low-risk population baseline against which comparisons made regarding relative levels perceived threat posed respective
